title: ED pelvic pain documentation
source_review_basis: full authoritative documents inspected and archived; item-level retain/rewrite/remove/add comparisons recorded

assessment.text: Specifies pain pattern, menstrual, bladder, bowel, movement, posture, function and medication history plus abdominal and pelvic examination for focal tenderness or structural findings.

source.title: The Initial Management of Chronic Pelvic Pain — Green-top Guideline No. 41
source.population: Women presenting for the first time with chronic pelvic pain of at least 6 months that is not pregnancy-related.
source.setting: General gynaecology and primary-care referral assessment.
source.exclusions: Exact for the chronic non-pregnancy subset only; old, international, and partial for acute, pregnancy-related, infectious, or emergency pelvic-pain presentations.
source.evidence_paraphrase: Specifies pain pattern, menstrual, bladder, bowel, movement, posture, function and medication history plus abdominal and pelvic examination for focal tenderness or structural findings.

assessment.text: Defines chronic pelvic pain as a symptom rather than a diagnosis and frames initial assessment around the woman’s concerns, function, and multifactorial context.

source.title: The Initial Management of Chronic Pelvic Pain — Green-top Guideline No. 41
source.population: Women presenting for the first time with chronic pelvic pain of at least 6 months that is not pregnancy-related.

source.evidence_paraphrase: Specifies pain pattern, menstrual, bladder, bowel, movement, posture, function and medication history plus abdominal and pelvic examination for focal tenderness or structural findings.

assessment.text: Specifies pain pattern, menstrual, bladder, bowel, movement, posture, function and medication history plus abdominal and pelvic examination for focal tenderness or structural findings.
assessment.rationale: The exact RCOG chronic pelvic-pain history and examination section supports onset, duration, severity, impact, associated symptoms, relevant negatives, patient concerns, risk context and manual focused examination in women with chronic pain; it does not cover all acute ED pelvic pain.

source.title: The Initial Management of Chronic Pelvic Pain — Green-top Guideline No. 41
source.population: Women presenting for the first time with chronic pelvic pain of at least 6 months that is not pregnancy-related.
source.setting: General gynaecology and primary-care referral assessment.
source.exclusions: Exact for the chronic non-pregnancy subset only; old, international, and partial for acute, pregnancy-related, infectious, or emergency pelvic-pain presentations.
source.evidence_paraphrase: Specifies pain pattern, menstrual, bladder, bowel, movement, posture, function and medication history plus abdominal and pelvic examination for focal tenderness or structural findings.

assessment.text: Lists pain, amenorrhoea, bleeding, gastrointestinal symptoms, dizziness or syncope, shoulder-tip pain, urinary symptoms, tenderness, haemodynamic signs, pregnancy testing, and escalation criteria.
assessment.rationale: When pregnancy is possible or known, the exact NICE section supports recording vital data, time-critical symptoms and clinician-decided escalation without asserting ectopic pregnancy or miscarriage.

source.title: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage: diagnosis and initial management
source.population: Women, trans men, and non-binary people with pain, bleeding, or other complications in early pregnancy up to 13 completed weeks.
source.setting: Primary, emergency, gynaecology, and early pregnancy assessment services.
source.exclusions: Exact for early-pregnancy pain, bleeding, ectopic-pregnancy warning signs, ultrasound, and hCG assessment; partial for uncomplicated pregnancy symptoms and not directly validated for UAE pathways.
source.evidence_paraphrase: Lists pain, amenorrhoea, bleeding, gastrointestinal symptoms, dizziness or syncope, shoulder-tip pain, urinary symptoms, tenderness, haemodynamic signs, pregnancy testing, and escalation criteria.

assessment.text: Defines clinician-performed transvaginal or selected transabdominal ultrasound and cautions against overinterpreting one early scan or LMP alone.
assessment.rationale: When early pregnancy applies, the exact ultrasound section supports recording imaging or test results already performed or reviewed, without recommending or interpreting them.

source.title: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage: diagnosis and initial management
source.population: Women, trans men, and non-binary people with pain, bleeding, or other complications in early pregnancy up to 13 completed weeks.
source.setting: Primary, emergency, gynaecology, and early pregnancy assessment services.
source.exclusions: Exact for early-pregnancy pain, bleeding, ectopic-pregnancy warning signs, ultrasound, and hCG assessment; partial for uncomplicated pregnancy symptoms and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_location.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1–1.7.6 — ultrasound for pregnancy location and viability

source.evidence_paraphrase: Defines clinician-performed transvaginal or selected transabdominal ultrasound and cautions against overinterpreting one early scan or LMP alone.

assessment.text: Defines chronic pelvic pain as a symptom rather than a diagnosis and frames initial assessment around the woman’s concerns, function, and multifactorial context.
assessment.rationale: The exact initial-assessment section supports a clinician-entered plan, completed discussion and arranged follow-up only when documented; it does not generate management.

source.title: The Initial Management of Chronic Pelvic Pain — Green-top Guideline No. 41
source.population: Women presenting for the first time with chronic pelvic pain of at least 6 months that is not pregnancy-related.
source.setting: General gynaecology and primary-care referral assessment.
source.exclusions: Exact for the chronic non-pregnancy subset only; old, international, and partial for acute, pregnancy-related, infectious, or emergency pelvic-pain presentations.

exact_location.heading: 1. Purpose and Scope; 2. Background; 5. Initial Assessment

source.evidence_paraphrase: Defines chronic pelvic pain as a symptom rather than a diagnosis and frames initial assessment around the woman’s concerns, function, and multifactorial context.

assessment.text: Supports clinician-selected STI sampling when PID is suspected and transvaginal ultrasound for adnexal masses and selected chronic pelvic-pain pathology.
assessment.rationale: Investigation recommendations were reviewed but not mapped to automatic tests.
